speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number two. This is an interview with Rose Winterfeldt. Now we're getting into 1938. How did events transpire for your family in 1938?

speaker: interviewee
text: In April of 1938 I had to leave the public school because all Jewish children were ordered by law to enter Jewish schools. And I-- my parents registered me with the Jewish middle school in the inner city of Berlin. It's the famous school Grosse Hamburger Strasse. And I started in April of '38 to go to that school. Now, we still lived in the outskirts, and I had to commute. I had to take two trains, and the whole trip was an hour and 15 minutes. Now, don't forget I was 10 years old, and I had to do that alone every morning and every afternoon. But when I came to the Jewish school, that was heaven. I was among my peers. Nobody pushed me around or called me names or did any-- any-- any-- everybody was nice. And to me as a 10-year-old, that was paradise. And I made friends. I--I-- I had, all of the sudden, children to share my life with, so to speak. And that was-- was great. It was great. Great teachers. Very good teachers and an excellent education, excellent Jewish education and also an excellent overall education. And I was happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other children who had a big commute the way you did, or were most of them from the area?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, most were from the city. I was actually, as far as I remember, the only one so far outside of the city. Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you wanted to visit with the children, did you-- were you able to since you lived so far away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they came on the weekends. They came to visit me because for them it was great to visit somebody who had a little garden outside of the city. So the kids came to my place. Almost every Saturday I had somebody visiting. And my mom, of course, was always willing and happy to have children visit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family-- let's say, let's go through into 1938-- still able to observe Judaism in the home and attend synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. Absolutely, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Synagogues were still available to people?

speaker: interviewee
text: All synagogues were still available. And, of course, in the home we had our seders. And on the high holy days, the whole family gathered in our house because my mother was the best cook. And so that was all observed and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Aside from you experiencing the problems you had in public school, did your parents experience problems outside the home during this period of time? I know your father lost his job, but I'm talking about personal harassment on the streets or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, not that I know of. No, I don't know that. I was the only one beaten up. No, no, there were not. Never. Not that I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, we are going through 1938. You're in the Jewish school, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: The next terrible event is Kristallnacht. Now, we lived, as I said, on the outskirts. I traveled by train into the city on the morning of November 10, and the train passed one particular synagogue. And I looked out, and I saw it burning. And I thought to myself, oh, how horrible and that an accident happened and the synagogue is burning. And I was standing by the door, by the train door, and looked out. And there was, across from me, a man standing, a young man. And he had as smirk and smile on his face. And that made me suspicious. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when I left the train on my station-- and now, I was in the inner city where there were Jewish stores and there was a Jewish cafe right in that area, in the train station area-- everything was smashed to pieces. The glass all over the street. The chairs of that little cafe were out on the street. And I walked through there, and I walked through there. And I walked toward my school. But I couldn't go all the way because the teachers had built, like, a barrier, and 'turn around. Go home. Go home.' They all sent us home, not to come closer to the school. And I went home, and I told my mother. And she said, that can't be. That can't be true. And she went to our Swedish neighbors, and they turned on their Swedish radio. And then we heard what had happened because this was not publicized right away. It wasn't publicized at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And nothing had happened in your area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing in my area. Even the Jewish Doctor Jacobson who lived up the street, nothing had happened to him. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then on that evening, my father disappeared. I mean, we-- he went to my grandparents to hide because they said all men will be arrested, you know. There were so many rumors. We contacted our Jewish friends, the family, everybody got on the phone talking, you know, what to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father went to my grandparents and-- to hide. So first of all, out of the house so that they couldn't arrest him right away. But, of course, my father would never have been arrested because he was poor. He had nothing they could take from him, material-wise. Only the rich Jews were arrested. Most of them were wealthy people. And then, we stayed home one day, and then the next day we went back to school and picked up the pieces and cleared away the glass. They had smashed all the windows in the school, you know. And there, right next to the school, was the oldest cemetery in Berlin, the cemetery where Moses Mendelssohn was buried. And, of course, they smashed his tombstone. And we had children build it up again and made his grave nice again. And life went on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were all the teachers back that day when you returned?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, they were all back. And all the children but many-- the fathers of many children were not. They were arrested, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a horrible time. I mean, we were 11-year-old children. We-- we behaved like 50-year-olds. We were mourning. We were sad. We were-- there was no laughter. There was nothing. It was horrible. It was horrible for children to live with this. What are they doing to us? How long did your father stay with your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think a week. I think a week because then the rumors came, and they won't harm the-- certain group, you know. And it died down, you know. They-- they-- they arrested only during that night, during the night of November 9 to 10, they arrested. Then they didn't arrest anybody anymore right at that point. So he came back home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so what was his thinking now after Kristallnacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother was pushing and pushing and pushing. Well, we tried to get out-- out-- out of Germany. And, of course, America was the dream. Everybody wanted to go to America, although my mother said, I'll go to Madagascar. I don't care. But my father registered us to immigrate to-- to the United States with the American consulate. And, and-- well, our number was too high. We never got out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall what your mother's parents were-- were feeling at this time or thinking at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my-- my-- my-- my father stayed with them, but my grandma came to us every day to check on us-- to check on my mom and my other grandma and myself that we are OK, you know. Every day she came out and check on us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now following Kristallnacht, was that the end of your family's being able to attend any kind of communal Jewish services?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: You still could go to it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. First of all, not all synagogues were destroyed because many-- many synagogues were built in the middle of apartment houses. And if they would have set fire to the synagogues, they would have burned their own people out. So these synagogues were spared. And services started again right away. And we went to synagogue. School continued. Jewish schools continued, and Jewish life continued.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then, how soon after Kristallnacht did you have to move?

speaker: interviewee
text: In April of '39-- now the development where we lived in Marienfelde, it was owned by the Catholic church. There's a story I really should tell you about that. And they-- they didn't renew the lease. So we had to move. By April of '39 we had to move out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they just waited till the lease ran out, and then because you were Jewish, it wasn't renewed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it wasn't renewed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you want to mention about the Catholic church?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- in the development was a little Catholic church, of course. And on Saturdays when there was a wedding, the bells would ring. And the children and even myself, being a little girl, would run there to see the bride. And I was standing there one day and watching or waiting for the bride to come, when a priest came out of the church and chased me away. You, Jew, leave, he said to me. A priest, in his-- is his robe, or whatever you want to call this. Yeah, chased me away. That-- by the by, I never set foot on that ground again, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were a little girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a little girl. My god, I could have been seven, eight, the most. Because once I went to the Jewish school, I wouldn't even go there, you know. But I was a little girl. I know I wore play-- a little play pants-- pants. I remember how I was dressed when I was chased away from there. That was a shock to me that the priest chased me away. Hm. Anyhow, we were now talking about--

speaker: interviewer
text: Having to move.

speaker: interviewee
text: Having to move. And at that time, Jews could not move where they wanted to move. They had to contact the so-called Reichsvereinigung, which was an administration consisting of Jews by set-- but set up by the Gestapo. So you had to go to the Reichsvereinigung, you know. We have-- we need a place to stay. And they assigned us to a Jew house in the inner city into an apartment where there already was a family of four living. And my--my-- mother-- I wasn't with them, but my mother and father looked at-- we were supposed to get two rooms in that apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my mother and father looked at this. And my mother said, who can live here? This is inhuman, looking out into a terrible, ugly courtyard and living in the inner city. To her it was-- you can't live here.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the people who were already living in the apartment were lovely people. And they started talking and talking, and they're like my parents. And they talked them into it, and we-- and we moved there. I mean, we had no choice anyway. But my mother was very upset that she has to bring her little, young daughter into that inner city, you know, prostitutes on the street and drunks on the street. I mean, she wanted perfect world. And so we moved in. And when we moved in, we actually had no money to pay the rent. We had no money to pay the rent because the move cost money. And-- but these people were so kind and so good. Don't worry about it. We don't need the money. Just move in. And we were the best of friends, and we had a lovely-- the outside world was horrible. But in-- in this apartment, within our little world, we were the happiest people you can imagine. We celebrated all the holidays together. And when we couldn't go to the synagogue, my father would make the Shabbos service in the living room. And he would read from the Torah. He had a Torah at home, a sefer Torah. And it was a beautiful, strong Jewish life in all this terrible going-on on the outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, this was a family of four?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Father, mother, and two sons.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were the sons?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, grown. 21 and 26 at that time. Grown sons.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Deutsch.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how much space did the eight of you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, it was a rather big apartment actually. We had two rooms. One room for-- my grandma did not move with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, she didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my grandma said, I don't want to do this. And she had a sister in the Jewish old age home. And she moved with her sister, into the room where her sister lived so then she shared a room in the old age home. She wanted to do that. She felt that was better for her. And so we had two rooms. My parents had a room. And then we had one room in which I slept but in which we also ate and everything else, like living, sleeping quarters, I don't know what you want to call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many rooms did the Deutsches have?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Deutsches had a living room and a bedroom and-- and another small room for one son. And one son slept in the living room. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where-- was the kitchen shared by the two families?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kitchen, Yes, yes. One side was the Deutsch side. One side was the Lehmann side. They were-- they kept kosher. My mother still kept kosher. So it all worked out fine. My mother taught Mrs.-- Frau Deutsch some new tricks on being kosher. She wasn't so kosher, not as kosher as my mother was. [LAUGHTER] It was fun.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about a bathroom? Would the apartment have its own bathroom?

speaker: interviewee
text: It had a little bathroom, like a lavatory, in front of the apartment. And in the back was a full bathroom, which we used. But on the weekends, there was even hot water so then everybody used that for showers and baths and whatever. You know, we were one family. We did everything together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, the whole apartment building-- was everyone Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everyone-- everyone was Jewish, the whole apartment building.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had there been non-Jews living there before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: They had been moved out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes. They were all Jewish families.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the surrounding neighborhood-- was this all Jewish also?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. That-- there were Jewish houses in-between. But of course, after '38, after the Kristallnacht, you had no Jewish stores anymore anyway, you know. So the surroundings were not Jewish. I can't say that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So certain apartment buildings were designated as Jewish houses, and people had to live--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, and as much as I can assume that those were buildings belonging originally to Jews, that they were owned by Jews, you know. And that's probably how it came that they designated these houses as Jew houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- what month again did you move in? In April?

speaker: interviewee
text: In April of '39. April of '39, and now, I could walk to school, which was nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at this point, were any of the children starting to leave the school? Or was anyone able to emigrate?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember-- I remember only one boy were able-- left my class. Of course, within the school were probably more. But in my class, only one boy was able to go to America in '39. That I remember. None of the teachers left. None of the teachers. It was very hard to get out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how did you hear about the outbreak of the war in September?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a Sunday, September 1, 1939. And we had a radio, and we heard that Germany had invaded Poland. And my father's reaction was that's the end of Hitler. If he goes into a war, he will go kaput. He hoped that he would go kaput fast, but, of course, that didn't happen. Yeah, that's how we heard it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once the war started, how did that affect life for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think pretty soon after war-- the war started, ration cards were issued for all, for all the population. And-- and in the beginning, I think the first cards we got were the same as all the Germans got. But then the second set already had a J stamped on-- 'Jew.' And, oh, god, there were so many restrictions, you know. Then it-- after Kristallnacht, one restriction after the other. You could shop only between four and five in the afternoon. And everybody was doing forced-- forced labor. They weren't even home between four and five to do the shopping. So people like myself, being a school girl, being home between four and five, I did all the shopping for everybody. And so many were started. I don't even know where to start to tell you what restrictions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, in '39, your mother started to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother started to work, yes, as a part-time bookkeeper because my father had no-- no income to speak of. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] until-- I could look it up when he really-- he was put into forced labor already in '39, but a little later in the year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this forced labor didn't bring him much of an income.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, but-- it brought him some income, yeah. And he was actually very lucky when he was sent to this business to work there as a forced laborer. It turned out this was a painting outfit. They would go around and paint the air raid shelters with a certain paint that would light in the dark, you know, and all kinds of war paints jobs. I don't really know. But when this guy who-- who owns-- who owned that business saw my father's resume or whatever my father did before, he said, you can do more than just painting walls. You could work in my office. And that's what my father did. He was very lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was a German man?

speaker: interviewee
text: A German. A Nazi.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Nazi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was a member of the partei. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have a bunch of Jews and many Jews assigned to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had only Jews and-- and slave laborers from-- from-- from Holland, from Poland, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a whole, whole-- more and more people came. His-- his business bloomed, you know. But he was a good guy. He didn't harm anybody. I have to tell you more about him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he put your father to work--

speaker: interviewee
text: He put my father--

speaker: interviewer
text: --managing the office?

speaker: interviewee
text: Managing the office, yes. And my father learned a completely new trade, so to speak, you know. And he-- he-- he was very fortunate that he didn't have to do physical, heavy work because he was a small man, you know. I don't know that he could have done a lot of hard labor, you know, like his brother did and died there.

speaker: interviewer
text: His brother was assigned to hard labor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, hard labor. He had to work on the railroad tracks in the summer and in the heat and in the winter in the cold. He was a small man. He was very fragile. He got sick and died. And he was only 44 years old. Just from sheer exhaustion. The body couldn't take it any more.

speaker: interviewer
text: When a person was assigned to forced labor, they went there in the morning and returned to their own apartment in the evening.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the evening. That's true. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And about how many hours a day did they work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, 10 hours at least. I personally when I was then later in forced labor, I worked 12 hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was this assignment made? Did you just receive it in the mail-- the assignment? Or did you report somewhere?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you-- no, you received in the mail that you have to report to the Jewish employment office. I mean, they called it the Jewish employment office, but-- but it was not run by Jews or anything. But all the Jews had to go there-- one particular employment office. And you went, and you got-- and they told you, you go there. You go there. You go there. And you would go to Krups. You go to all these big names. You'd get Farben, you know. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and so my father was assigned to this guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your mother got the job as a bookkeeper, was she also assigned there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. That was-- she-- she had-- she was able to find a job on her own. But when she started there, or when she-- when she went for the interview there, she told the boss right away who she was and who her husband is and who her child is right away. And the man said, that doesn't matter. You can work for us. And he was very good to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this-- and this was also a Nazi that she was working for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't-- I wouldn't call him a Nazi, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he was a German man. And he was nice and helpful.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a German man. He has-- yes, he-- he had a business. Actually, it was a very famous stationery in the old city of Berlin. The name was very famous.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, between your mother and your father at this point, did you have enough money to get through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, now we could pay rent, and we could pay for the food that we got on-- on ration-- ration cards which was very little. It's another thing to talk about. But at least we had some money. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you mentioned some other restrictions. What were some of these other restrictions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were talking '39? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, of course, even earlier you couldn't go to the theaters. You couldn't go to the movies. You couldn't go to parks. All the park benches had signs on 'Not for Jews.' Couldn't sit in a park. Many, many restaurants had signs, 'Jews and dogs are not desired,' you know, or undesirable or whatever, you know. You couldn't go [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] anywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, Berlin has a beautiful surroundings with a lot of water. There are always boats. You wouldn't go on boats. I learned to ice skate in the winter of 1934. And my mother wanted me to learn how to swim in the summer of '35. And she took me to the local pool or swimming school or whatever, and the first question was 'Is that child Jewish?' And my mother said, yes. And well, we won't teach Jews. So I never learned how to swim. I still can't swim. See that's how it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it was every-- a little more, a little more, a little more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any memories of the '36 Olympics at all? Did that come to a child's attention at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were very-- I remember that we heard on the radio when Jesse Owens won. And that was-- that's all I remember really, you know. It didn't-- it wasn't of no interest to us at that time, you know. I remember Jesse Owens. I definitely do because my parents probably listened to the radio a little bit, you know. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because I had heard that--

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, I was nine years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: --some of the restrictions were lifted for the Olympics.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's-- that's true.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the park bench signs were taken down.

speaker: interviewee
text: The signs were down. That's absolutely true. The signs were taken down. Sure. Sure yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you go use the parks or anything at that point because the signs were gone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No way.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew you still couldn't, but it didn't show.

speaker: interviewee
text: No way. We wouldn't do that. We wouldn't. We were too proud. We were very proud, you know. Once you kick me, forget it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then you're not going back.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So and you were still going to school at the time-- let's say going back to the fall of '39. The war was starting. Did this, the fact that the war started, bring any changes in the school or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no, no changes in the school. It was still the same type community, you know. Everybody was Jewish. And that was fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And people were still in place at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they were '39, '40. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then-- then came '41 when we had to wear the star. In the meantime, our food rations had gone down, and we got very little food, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever get-- well, we'd better stop now, and I'll ask the question on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
